summary: This PR adds header validation to detect CR/LF injection in HTTP headers via a new `check_header_validity` function and two compiled regexes. While the feature is directionally correct, there are several significant gaps: the regex anchor `$` allows trailing `\n` to slip through (use `\Z` instead), header names are never validated (only values), and non-string/bytes values cause an unexpected `TypeError`. Additionally, the test suite has incomplete coverage of the bytes branch and of invalid inputs.

findings.title: Byte-branch of `check_header_validity` is not tested for invalid inputs
findings.detail: The test suite exercises only the `str` branch of `check_header_validity` for invalid header values. There is no negative test passing a `bytes` value containing `\r`, `\n`, or leading whitespace (e.g. `b'bar\r\nbaz'`, `b' bar'`) to confirm that `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE` correctly raises `InvalidHeader`. The happy-path byte test (`str.encode(u'fbbq')`) does not substitute for error-path coverage. Add dedicated negative tests for the bytes branch.

findings.title: Header name is never validated — CR/LF injection via header name is possible
findings.detail: The `check_header_validity` function only validates the header **value**; the header **name** is never checked against the regex. An attacker or misconfigured caller who controls a header name containing `\r` or `\n` (e.g. `{'X-Foo\r\nX-Injected: evil': 'value'}`) can inject arbitrary headers. The same (or a stricter) regex should be applied to the name. Both the correctness and security reviewers independently flagged this issue.

findings.title: Regex `$` anchor allows values ending with `\n` to bypass validation
Non-string/non-bytes header values crash with `TypeError` instead of `InvalidHeader`
findings.detail: The function has only two branches: `bytes` and else (treated as `str`). If `value` is `None`, an `int`, or any other non-string type, `pat.match(value)` raises a `TypeError` rather than `InvalidHeader`, causing an unexpected and unhandled exception rather than a clean validation error. Numeric values (e.g. `Content-Length: 0`) are legitimate in user code. The function should either convert `value` to a string before matching, or reject non-string/non-bytes values with an explicit `InvalidHeader` raise. There is also no test covering this path, leaving the behavior undocumented.

findings.title: Test labeled 'tab' uses spaces, not a tab character — tab case is untested
findings.detail: `headers_tab = {'foo': '   bar'}` contains three space characters, not a tab (`\t`). The comment and variable name both claim to test for a leading tab, but a tab is a distinct whitespace character and should be explicitly tested with `{'foo': '\tbar'}` to ensure the regex rejects it. Both the correctness and style reviewers flagged this.

findings.title: Headers added after `prepare_headers` are never validated
findings.detail: `check_header_validity` is only invoked inside `prepare_headers`. Headers subsequently mutated by auth handlers, adapters, or `merge_environment_settings` bypass validation entirely, leaving a residual injection path. Consider enforcing validation at `CaseInsensitiveDict.__setitem__` or re-running validation just before the request is sent.

findings.title: Partial `self.headers` state left on `InvalidHeader` exception mid-iteration
findings.detail: `self.headers = CaseInsensitiveDict()` is assigned unconditionally before the validation loop. If validation raises `InvalidHeader` partway through a multi-header dict, `self.headers` will be partially populated with the valid headers seen before the invalid one. Callers that catch the exception and reuse the `PreparedRequest` object may see incomplete header state. Consider accumulating headers into a local `dict` or generator and assigning to `self.headers` only after all entries pass validation.

findings.title: No regression test for `prepare_headers(None)` / `prepare_headers({})` after refactor
findings.detail: The refactored `prepare_headers` always initialises `self.headers = CaseInsensitiveDict()` unconditionally. While the behavior for `None`/empty input is unchanged, there is no dedicated regression test confirming `self.headers` is still correctly an empty dict when `headers` is falsy after the restructuring.

findings.title: Missing space after comma in test method signature
findings.detail: `def test_header_validation(self,httpbin):` is missing a space after the comma. All other test methods in the file use `(self, httpbin)`. Fix to `def test_header_validation(self, httpbin):`.

findings.title: Trailing whitespace on import line
findings.detail: Line 30 ends with a trailing space after the comma in the import statement (`to_native_string, `). Trim the trailing whitespace to match the style of surrounding import lines.

findings.title: Only one blank line between `check_header_validity` and `urldefragauth`
findings.detail: PEP 8 and the surrounding code style require two blank lines between top-level function definitions. There is only one blank line between `check_header_validity` and the next function `urldefragauth`. Add a second blank line.

findings.title: Unused `r =` assignment inside `pytest.raises` blocks
findings.detail: Inside each `with pytest.raises(InvalidHeader):` block the return value is assigned to `r` (e.g. `r = requests.get(...)`), but `r` is never used — the exception is expected before assignment completes. Drop the `r =` prefix for clarity and consistency with other tests in the file that use bare calls inside `pytest.raises`.

findings.title: Byte regex uses double-escaped string instead of raw byte string
findings.detail: `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE` is compiled with `b'^\\S[^\\r\\n]*$|^$'` (plain byte string with doubled backslashes) while `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_STR` uses a raw string `r'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$'`. For consistency and readability, use a raw byte string: `rb'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$'`.

findings.title: Missing blank line before new module-level regex constants
findings.detail: The style in `utils.py` consistently separates top-level definitions with a blank line. The comment and regex constants immediately follow `to_native_string` without a blank line. Add a blank line between the end of `to_native_string` and the new comment/constant block.

findings.title: Unnecessary intermediate `header` variable in loop
findings.detail: The loop currently unpacks into `header` only to immediately pass it to `check_header_validity(header)` and then re-unpack `name, value = header`. Unpacking directly in the `for` statement is cleaner: `for name, value in headers.items(): check_header_validity((name, value)); self.headers[to_native_string(name)] = value`.

findings.title: Regex rejects valid single-character header values that are non-whitespace
check_header_validity does not validate the header name
findings.detail: The function only validates the header value, but never validates the header name. A header name containing `\r` or `\n` (e.g. `{'foo\r\nX-Evil: injected': 'value'}`) would pass through without raising `InvalidHeader`. HTTP header injection can also occur via a malicious header name, not just the value. The name should be validated with the same or a similar regex.

findings.title: check_header_validity crashes with TypeError when value is not bytes or str
findings.detail: The function only has two branches: `bytes` and else (str). If `value` is an integer, a `None`, or another non-string type (which can happen when users pass `{'Connection': None}` to remove a header, or pass numeric values), the `pat.match(value)` call on the str regex will raise a `TypeError` rather than an `InvalidHeader`. The existing code in `prepare_headers` used to accept any value and pass it to `CaseInsensitiveDict`, so this is a behavior regression. At minimum, non-string/bytes types should either be explicitly handled (e.g. converted or rejected with `InvalidHeader`) rather than causing an unexpected `TypeError`.

findings.title: Regex pattern for bytes uses `re.match` with a bytes pattern but the string version could have re.DOTALL issues
findings.detail: The patterns are compiled without `re.DOTALL`, which is intentional (so `.` doesn't match newlines). However, the byte pattern `b'^\\S[^\\r\\n]*$|^$'` has the same trailing-newline issue with `$` as described above. In Python, `$` in a non-MULTILINE regex matches at end-of-string OR just before a trailing `\n`. So `b'foo\n'` would match `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE` and pass validation despite containing a newline. Use `re.compile(..., re.MULTILINE)` with `\Z` anchor, or use `\Z` instead of `$` to ensure a true end-of-string match.

findings.title: prepare_headers initializes self.headers before the validity check, leaving partial state on exception
findings.detail: The new code sets `self.headers = CaseInsensitiveDict()` unconditionally (line 407) before iterating and potentially raising `InvalidHeader`. If the headers dict has multiple entries and an invalid one appears partway through iteration, `self.headers` will be partially populated with the valid headers that came before it. Depending on whether the caller catches `InvalidHeader` and reuses the `PreparedRequest` object, this could be a source of confusion. Previously, headers were only set atomically (via a generator passed to `CaseInsensitiveDict`). Consider building the dict in a local variable and assigning to `self.headers` only after all validations pass.

findings.title: Test for tab leading whitespace uses spaces, not a tab character
findings.detail: The test `headers_tab = {'foo': '   bar'}` uses three space characters, not a tab character (`\t`). The test comment says "Test for tab" but doesn't actually test tab. A genuine tab (`\t`) is also whitespace and should be rejected. The test should use `{'foo': '\tbar'}` to test the tab case.

findings.title: Header injection regex does not validate the header name
findings.detail: Only the header value is validated against the regex. The header name (key) is never checked for CR/LF characters. An attacker who controls a header name could inject additional headers via a crafted name such as `'X-Foo\r\nX-Injected: evil'`. The `check_header_validity` function should apply a similar regex check to `name` as well as `value`.

findings.title: Regex allows lone CR (\r) in header value — incomplete injection protection
Non-string, non-bytes header values silently bypass validation
findings.detail: If `value` is neither `bytes` nor `str` (e.g., an `int`, a custom object), `pat` is set to the str regex but `pat.match(value)` will raise a `TypeError` rather than performing a meaningful security check. Numeric header values are legitimately used (e.g., `Content-Length: 0`). The function should either convert the value to a string before matching, or explicitly handle non-string types, to avoid both bypasses and unexpected TypeErrors.

findings.title: check_header_validity only called during prepare_headers — headers added later are not validated
findings.detail: Headers can be added or mutated on `PreparedRequest.headers` after `prepare_headers` is called (e.g., by auth handlers, adapters, or merge_environment_settings). Those later-added headers never pass through `check_header_validity`, leaving a residual injection path. The validation should ideally be enforced at a lower layer (e.g., by overriding `CaseInsensitiveDict.__setitem__`) or repeated before the request is sent.

findings.title: Missing blank line before module-level constants
findings.detail: The existing style in utils.py consistently separates top-level definitions with a blank line. The comment and two regex constants at lines 735–737 immediately follow `to_native_string` without a blank line separator. Add a blank line between the end of `to_native_string` and this comment block.

findings.title: Missing blank line between `check_header_validity` and `urldefragauth`
findings.detail: Every other top-level function in utils.py is separated from its neighbour by two blank lines (consistent with PEP 8 and the surrounding style). There is only one blank line between `check_header_validity` and `urldefragauth` (line 754). Add a second blank line.

findings.title: Raw string not used for byte regex, inconsistent with string regex
findings.detail: `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE` is compiled with a plain byte string `b'^\\S[^\\r\\n]*$|^$'` while `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_STR` uses a raw string `r'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$'`. For consistency (and to avoid the double-backslash noise) the byte pattern should also be a raw byte string: `rb'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$'`.

findings.title: Trailing whitespace on import line
findings.detail: Line 30 ends with a trailing space after the comma (`to_native_string, `). The surrounding import lines have no trailing whitespace; this should be trimmed.

findings.title: Intermediate `header` variable is unnecessary
findings.detail:
The loop unpacks into `header` only to immediately call `check_header_validity(header)` and then re-unpack `name, value = header`. Unpacking directly in the `for` statement and passing a tuple to the check function — or unpacking first — would be cleaner and match the terse style of the surrounding code:
```python
for name, value in headers.items():
    check_header_validity((name, value))
    self.headers[to_native_string(name)] = value
```

findings.title: Missing space after comma in method signature
findings.detail: `def test_header_validation(self,httpbin):` is missing a space after the comma. All other test methods in the file use `(self, httpbin)` with a space.

findings.title: Unused variable `r` assigned inside `pytest.raises` blocks
findings.detail: Inside each `with pytest.raises(InvalidHeader):` block the result is assigned to `r` (e.g. `r = requests.get(...)`), but `r` is never read — the exception is expected to be raised before assignment completes. The surrounding test code (e.g. `test_invalid_url`) uses bare calls without assignment. Drop the `r =` prefix for consistency and clarity.

findings.title: Tab comment inaccurate — value contains spaces, not a tab
findings.detail: `headers_tab = {'foo': '   bar'}` uses three spaces, but the inline comment on line 1165 says "Test for tab". Either use an actual tab character (`\t`) or update the comment and variable name to say "spaces".

findings.title: Byte-string header values with invalid characters not tested
findings.detail: The `check_header_validity` function has two branches: one for `bytes` values (using `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE`) and one for `str` values (using `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_STR`). The tests in `test_header_no_return_chars` and `test_header_no_leading_space` only exercise the `str` branch. There is no test that passes a `bytes` header value containing `\r`, `\n`, or a leading space/whitespace to verify the byte-regex branch correctly raises `InvalidHeader`.

findings.title: No test for byte header value with leading whitespace raising InvalidHeader
findings.detail: `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE` covers the byte-value path. The `test_header_validation` test does include a valid byte value (`str.encode(u'fbbq')`), but no test covers a *invalid* byte header value (e.g. `b' bar'` or `b'bar\r\nbaz'`) to ensure `InvalidHeader` is raised. This leaves the negative/error path of the byte branch completely untested.

findings.title: prepare_headers called with None/empty headers not explicitly tested after refactor
findings.detail: The refactored `prepare_headers` now always initialises `self.headers = CaseInsensitiveDict()` before the `if headers:` block. The prior code also had an `else` branch doing the same. The behaviour when `headers=None` or `headers={}` is passed is unchanged but there is no dedicated regression test exercising the new code path to confirm `self.headers` is still correctly set to an empty dict when `headers` is falsy.

findings.title: Header value of non-string, non-bytes type (e.g. integer) not tested
findings.detail: `check_header_validity` will call `pat.match(value)` where `pat` is a string regex. If `value` is neither `bytes` nor `str` (e.g. an integer), the `str` regex branch is taken and `pat.match()` will raise a `TypeError` rather than `InvalidHeader`. There is no test exercising this edge case to confirm intended behaviour.

findings.title: test_header_validation does not test byte header values with invalid characters
findings.detail: The `test_header_validation` happy-path test includes one byte value (`str.encode(u'fbbq')`) but only asserts the string header value `foo` was transmitted correctly. It does not assert that the byte header `qux` was also transmitted correctly, and more importantly no corresponding negative test verifies that an invalid byte header value raises `InvalidHeader`.

findings.title: Byte-branch of `check_header_validity` is not tested for invalid inputs
findings.detail: The test suite exercises only the `str` branch of `check_header_validity` for invalid header values. There is no negative test passing a `bytes` value containing `\r`, `\n`, or leading whitespace (e.g. `b'bar\r\nbaz'`, `b' bar'`) to confirm that `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_BYTE` correctly raises `InvalidHeader`. The happy-path byte test (`str.encode(u'fbbq')`) does not substitute for error-path coverage. Add dedicated negative tests for the bytes branch.

findings.title: Header name is never validated — CR/LF injection via header name is possible
findings.detail: The `check_header_validity` function only validates the header **value**; the header **name** is never checked against the regex. An attacker or misconfigured caller who controls a header name containing `\r` or `\n` (e.g. `{'X-Foo\r\nX-Injected: evil': 'value'}`) can inject arbitrary headers. The same (or a stricter) regex should be applied to the name. Both the correctness and security reviewers independently flagged this issue.

There is also no test covering this path, leaving the behavior undocumented.

Both the correctness and style reviewers flagged this.

findings.detail: `def test_header_validation(self,httpbin):` is missing a space after the comma. All other test methods in the file use `(self, httpbin)`. Fix to `def test_header_validation(self, httpbin):`.
